title: [Security]: /metrics endpoint default-unauthenticated exposes multi-tenant PII in production deployments
body:
## Summary

The `/metrics` Prometheus endpoint is **unauthenticated by default** and exposes sensitive multi-tenant data in production LiteLLM Proxy deployments. While an opt-in authentication mechanism exists (`require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: true`), the insecure default leads to widespread real-world exposure.

This is a follow-up to #13644, which was auto-closed by the stale bot as `NOT_PLANNED` without any maintainer response — despite multiple users confirming the security impact.

## Vulnerability Details

**Type:** Information Disclosure / Tenant Isolation Bypass  
**Default behavior:** `/metrics` endpoint serves ~2MB of Prometheus metrics to any unauthenticated HTTP request  
**CVSS 3.1:** 7.5 (High) — `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N`

## Data Exposed via `litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric` Labels

The Prometheus metrics include per-request labels that leak the following data for **every tenant**:

| Label | Sensitive Data |
|-------|---------------|
| `hashed_api_key` | SHA-256 hash of customer API keys |
| `api_key_alias` | Human-readable key names (often containing company names) |
| `team` | Team UUIDs |
| `team_alias` | Company names and **employee email addresses** (PII) |
| `end_user` | End-user identifiers |
| `user` | User identifiers with email addresses |
| `requester_ip_address` | Client IP addresses revealing infrastructure topology |
| `user_agent` | Full user-agent strings (exposing Azure Logic Apps workflow IDs, automation tools) |
| `model` | AI models accessed per customer |
| `model_id` | Internal model UUIDs |

## Real-World Impact (Verified)

During authorized security testing, we identified production LiteLLM Proxy deployments where the unauthenticated `/metrics` endpoint exposed:

- **15+ customer tenants** with complete operational telemetry
- **Corporate email addresses** in `team_alias` fields (GDPR-relevant PII)
- **Client IP addresses** enabling infrastructure geolocation and cloud provider identification
- **Azure Logic Apps workflow IDs** in user-agent strings, enabling reverse engineering of customer automation infrastructure
- **API key hashes** with aliases mapping to specific companies
- **Cross-tenant request patterns** enabling competitive intelligence gathering

A single unauthenticated GET request returns the complete dataset for all tenants.

## Root Cause

The `/metrics` endpoint is registered without any authentication middleware by default. The existing `require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint` setting is:

1. **Opt-in** rather than opt-out (insecure by default)
2. **Not prominently documented** in deployment guides
3. **Not enabled in the default/example configurations**

This violates the principle of **secure by default** — especially critical for a multi-tenant proxy handling API credentials.

## Recommendation

1. **Make authentication the default** for `/metrics` — require explicit opt-out (`allow_unauthenticated_metrics: true`) rather than opt-in authentication
2. **Sanitize metric labe

title: [Minter] Add solo staking
body:
Users should be able to use our contract for solo staking. 
To start we would require users to have the following: 
- 32 ETH
- 1000 SGT bonded and timelocked in a way that admins can unlock it when the users exit their validators or slash it if the user acts in a nefarious way.   

For this we will need to upgrade the send2eth2 function in the minter to use AccessControl over having a single owner.  

For a simplified implementation, the SGT bonded user should be able to call the function directly, resulting in a deposit function call which returns 32 vETH2 to them in exchange for 32.1 ETH.  

The solo staker can then use their vETH2 to earn profits at a higher APY in our ecosystem while waiting for ETH2 rewards to be unlocked via a chain merge or other mechanism the EF comes up with.  

This way the solo staker holds onto both their private keys for withdrawals and the validator. 

The solo staker will be responsible for running their own validator to start with and entering the correct public keys for the call registering the validator.

title: Bounty: Implement InkCanvas and InkPresenter controls (lifetime commercial license reward)
body:
## Bounty
Whoever fully implements `InkCanvas` + `InkPresenter` parity for this repository will receive a **lifetime commercial license** for InkkSlinger.

## Why this issue is combined
`InkPresenter` is the stroke-rendering primitive hosted by `InkCanvas`. In WPF terms they are tightly coupled. In this repository, implementing them together in one PR avoids partial API surfaces that are hard to validate.

## Target outcome
Deliver a practical MVP parity slice where users can:
- place an `InkCanvas` in XML/XAML,
- draw strokes with pointer input,
- see strokes rendered and clipped correctly,
- keep behavior deterministic with focused regression tests,
- keep full solution + test suite green.

## Repository orientation (start here)
Read these files first:
- `README.md`
- `TODO.md`
- `UI-FOLDER-MAP.md`

### High-value local references for implementation patterns
- Input routing and focus/capture orchestration:
  - `UI/Managers/Root/Services/UiRootInputPipeline.cs`
  - `UI/Input/Core/FocusManager.cs`
- Base control and rendering lifecycle:
  - `UI/Controls/Base/UIElement.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Base/Control.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Base/FrameworkElement.cs`
- Pointer-handling control examples:
  - `UI/Controls/Inputs/RichTextBox.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Scrolling/ScrollViewer.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Inputs/Slider.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Containers/DocumentViewer.cs`
- Rendering helpers:
  - `UI/Rendering/Core/UiDrawing.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Primitives/Shape.cs`
- XAML object creation and property-element plumbing:
  - `UI/Xaml/Core/XamlLoader.cs`
- Schema authoring:
  - `Schemas/InkkSlinger.UI.xsd`
- Recently added reference pattern for introducing a new control end-to-end:
  - `UI/Controls/Containers/DocumentViewer.cs`
  - `InkkSlinger.Tests/DocumentViewer*Tests.cs`

## Suggested implementation architecture
This is a recommended shape, not a strict requirement.

### 1) Core ink model types (new)
Add small, testable model types under `UI/Text/Viewing/` or `UI/Input/` or another coherent location (maintainer discretion):
- `InkStroke`
  - `IReadOnlyList<Vector2>` points
  - stroke color/thickness/opacity
  - optional bounding box cache
- `InkStrokeCollection`
  - add/remove/clear
  - change notifications for invalidation hooks
- optional `InkDrawingAttributes` style object if you want WPF-like configuration

Keep allocations controlled on pointer-move hot paths.

### 2) `InkPresenter` control (new)
`InkPresenter` should be the rendering primitive for stroke collections:
- derive from `Control`
- expose DP(s) for stroke source / stroke visual settings
- render strokes in `OnRender(SpriteBatch)`
- support clipping (`TryGetClipRect`) consistent with surrounding controls
- invalidate minimally when stroke collection mutates

### 3) `InkCanvas` control (new)
`InkCanvas` should host `InkPresenter` and own interaction semantics:
- derive from `Control` (or container base if required)
- expose DP(s) for stroke collection and editing mode
- pointer interaction path:
  - pointe

title: Title: Invalid threshold input (“$ of X”) triggers fallback mode that duplicates entropy and leaks checksum-index hiding mechanism (significant implementation bug)
body:
Hi Bitaps team,
I’m a self-taught BTC learner who has been testing your Mnemonic Secret Sharing tool at [bitaps.com](http://bitaps.com/) (the one linked from the 1 BTC Shamir challenge page). While playing with the “Mnemonic secret sharing schema threshold” form I discovered unexpected behavior that appears to be a significant implementation bug.
Steps to reproduce:
1.  Go to the Mnemonic secret sharing tool.
2.  In the input box, paste any 12-word mnemonic (I tested both my own phrases and the two public challenge shares concatenated as one 24-word string).
3.  Set the threshold to something invalid like $ of 5 or $ of 8 (or any non-numeric value in the first box).
4.  Click “Calculate”.
5.  Repeat “Calculate” several times.
Expected behavior:
The tool should either reject the invalid threshold with a clear error (“threshold must be a number between 1 and 255”) or refuse to generate shares, since proper Shamir requires a valid integer threshold.
Actual behavior (what I observed every time):
•  The tool silently generates the exact number of “shares” I typed in the “of X” box (5, 8, etc.).
•  Every share is identical in the first 11 words (the entropy part is simply duplicated).
•  Only the 12th word (checksum) changes.
•  Repeatedly hitting Calculate makes the 12th word cycle alphabetically through valid BIP39 words (example cycle I captured on multiple runs): funny → fury → furnace → future → gadget → gain → galaxy → gap → garlic → garment → game → garden → garage → garbage → etc.
•  Changing the last word of my input phrase (e.g. to something starting with “d”) shifts the entire cycle to words starting with that letter.
•  The cycle length seems capped around 16 possibilities, which matches the 4 checksum bits described in your BIP/mnemonic-improvement.md.
This happens with any input, including the two public challenge shares you published.
Why this is a bug (and why it matters for the challenge): Your own documentation explains that share indices are hidden in the 4 checksum bits of the last word so every share still looks like a normal BIP39 mnemonic. In normal operation (valid 3-of-5 threshold) the tool should run real GF(256) Shamir polynomials and produce completely different entropy in each share.
When the threshold is invalid, it falls back to this trivial “duplicate + cycle checksum for index” mode. This exposes the index-hiding trick in plain view and shows that input validation is missing (NaN checks are not handled, so the code skips the real sharing logic).
This matches the “significant implementation bug” category in your bug bounty (0.05 BTC+ tier) and could potentially weaken share indistinguishability or allow reconstruction tricks from fewer than the required shares.
Impact:
•  Makes the tool behave unpredictably on bad input.
•  Reveals internal mechanics that should stay hidden.
•  Possibly related to why no one has claimed the main 1 BTC challenge yet (the fallback could be exploitable).
Suggested fix:
•  Add proper type-c

title: Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)
body:
# Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)

Implement an optimized LWT and ILWT kernels for TT-Metal/TTNN on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware, which would enable usage of discrete wavelet transforms (DWTs) on device.

## 📝 Background

Discrete wavelet transform will help us enable multi-resolution signal processing directly on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware. This unlocks:
- JPEG2000 lossy compression.
- Real-time Digital Signal Processing pipelines.
- Audio, biosignals, radar, and seismic data processing applications.

## 🎯 What Success Looks Like

Requirements
- Support 1D/2D inputs with batch dimension (B, 1, H, W).
  - Layout can be both row major and tile. Preferably RM for 1d input, and tile layout for 2d input.
- Support the wavelets from PyWavelets catalog:
  - Haar: db1
  - Daubechies: dbN
  - Biorthogonal (used in JPEG2000): biorN.M
  - Symlets: symN
  - Coiflets: coifN
  - Discrete Meyer: dmey
- Support fp32 (SFPU-based implementation)
- Should be implemented as ttnn operation (`ttnn.dwt`/`ttnn.idwt`)
  - Should be implemented as optimized multicore kernel (not a naive composite version)
- Precision targets for the SFPU fp32 (Wavelet categories are defined in [the table below](#wavelet-categories)):
- Compact lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `tap_size <= 12`, `num_steps <= 11`, and `max_abs_coeff < 1e2`.
  - Forward transformation precision should be measured against PyWavelets using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-7;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-5.
  - Reconstruction precision should be measured against the original signal using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-7;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-5.

- Medium lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `12 < tap_size <= 24`, `12 <= num_steps <= 23`, and `max_abs_coeff < 1e4`.
  - Forward transformation precision should be measured against PyWavelets using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-6;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-4.
  - Reconstruction precision should be measured against the original signal using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-6;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-4.

- Large / numerically sensitive lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `tap_size > 24`, or `num_steps > 23`, or `max_abs_coeff >= 1e4`.
  - Forward precision is best effort.
  - Reconstruction precision is best effort.
  - These wavelets should report per-wavelet median / p95 / max L2 relative error and known outliers, but should not be treated as hard-gating failures.

- Precision reports should always include a per-wavelet breakdown and explicitly call out known SFPU fp32 outliers.


- No strict requirements for potential lower precision implementation.

- Optionally
  - Investigate FPU implementation (im2col/conv based) with tf32/bf16 + acc fp32 precision
  - Support l1 sharded inputs
  - Support arbitraty number of

title: [Feature Request]: Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform and Inverse LWT
body:
# Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)

**By** @draklowell, @ke1rro, @kerfmanner, @sodaqqq *@ Ukrainian Catholic University*

## Summary
We target to implement an optimized LWT and ILWT kernels for TT-Metal to enable O(N) multi-resolution signal processing directly on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware. This unlocks efficient wavelet-based AI architectures (WaveKAN), JPEG2000 compression, and real-time Digital Signal Processing pipelines without CPU fallback. The implementation will be memory-aware and optimized for Tensix L1 constraints and NoC topology.

## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

While work on Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is currently in progress within the tt-metal ecosystem ([#21412](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/21412)), FFT alone is insufficient for modern signal processing tasks dealing with **non-stationary signals**. FFT provides frequency information but completely loses temporal localization - it cannot tell _when_ a particular frequency event occurred.

The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) applications are vast and critical for many domains:

- **Advanced Digital Signal Processing**: Processing audio, biosignals, radar, and seismic data where _when_ a frequency occurs is as important as _which_ frequency. Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) attempts to solve this but suffers from fixed resolution (Heisenberg uncertainty principle) and higher computational cost.
- **Next-Gen AI Models**: Emerging architectures like **WaveKAN** (Wavelet Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks) replace standard activation functions with wavelet bases to improve convergence, interpretability, and function approximation. Currently, there are no native ops to accelerate these models on Wormhole/Blackhole.
- **Signal Compression**: Wavelet transforms are the foundation of JPEG 2000 and modern audio codecs, offering superior compression without block artifacts compared to DCT-based methods.
- **Denoising**: Wavelet thresholding is one of the most effective techniques for noise removal across domains - from medical imaging to financial time series.

## Describe the solution you'd like

We propose implementing Discrete Wavelet Transform via **LWT** and **ILWT** as a logical extension to the DSP capabilities being built around FFT.

Our team has been exploring the **Lifting Scheme** (Sweldens, 1996) as the implementation approach.

The Lifting Scheme decomposes the transform into three simple steps that are done in-place:
- Split: Separate even/odd indexed samples.
- Predict: Predict odd samples from even (generates detail coefficients).
- Update: Update even samples (generates approximation coefficients).

![Lifting scheme (from Wikipedia)](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b011cc22-0709-4bb9-a8df-8586bc583ab4)

### Why Lifting Scheme?

We believe this is the right fit for Tenstorrent hardware for the following reasons:
| Property | Fast Wavelet Transform | Lifting Wavelet Transform |
|--

title: [Security #5] Unsafe Deserialization in Mint Cache (High)
body:
---
title: "Security #5: Unsafe Deserialization in Mint Cache (High)"
labels: ["security", "bug", "mint", "cache", "deserialization"]
assignees: []
---

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #5

**Severity:** High  
**Component:** Mint Cache Layer  
**File:** `cashu/mint/cache.py` (line 54)  
**Impact:** Potential RCE, data manipulation, cache poisoning  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925 (same researcher - ongoing audit)

---

## Summary

The mint cache layer uses `json.loads()` on data retrieved from Redis without proper validation. If an attacker can inject malicious data into the Redis cache, they could manipulate mint operations or potentially execute arbitrary code.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/mint/cache.py`  
**Line:** 54

```python
async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    # ...
    resp = await self.redis.get(key)
    if resp:
        return json.loads(resp)  # ← VULNERABLE: Unsafe deserialization
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Found no cached response for key {key}")
```

### The Bug

Data retrieved from Redis is directly deserialized with `json.loads()` without:
1. Validating the data structure
2. Checking data integrity
3. Verifying the source

### Attack Vector

1. Attacker gains ability to write to Redis (direct access, SSRF, etc.)
2. Injects malicious serialized data
3. Mint reads and deserializes the data
4. Attacker controls mint behavior

---

## Impact

**Severity:** High

**Potential Impact:**
- Mint cache poisoning
- Manipulation of mint operations
- Potential for RCE if combined with other vulnerabilities
- Loss of funds if quote/proof data is manipulated

**Affected Users:**
- All mint users
- Wallet holders

---

## Recommended Fix

### Validate Data Structure Before Deserialization

```python
async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    resp = await self.redis.get(key)
    if resp:
        # Validate data structure before deserializing
        try:
            data = json.loads(resp)
            # Add type/structure validation here
            if not isinstance(data, dict):
                raise ValueError("Invalid cache data type")
            return data
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            logger.error(f"Invalid JSON in cache for key {key}")
            raise
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Found no cached response for key {key}")
```

---

## Affected Versions

- Nutshell v0.20.0 (confirmed)
- Earlier versions (likely affected)

---

## Bounty Information

**Requested Payout:** Cashu ecash or Lightning Bitcoin  
**Estimated Value:** $1,000-$3,000 (High severity)  

**My Lightning Address:**  
```
npub1sh9jm4ztw9a9g4wnqfacd4749r54agxastujhs6t2r0lgel0a7esjkd4fz@npub.cash
```

---

## Disclosure Timeline

- **2026-03-24:** Vulnerability discovered by automated scanner
- **2026-03-25:** Reported to Cashu team (Issue #926)
- **2026-04-XX:** Planned public disclosure (90 days)

---

### Executive Summary
Gitcoin Grants pioneered the use of Quadratic Funding (QF) at scale, turning a cryptographic trick into a sustainable public goods funding engine. Over 300 rounds, it distributed more than $50M to thousands of open source projects, from Ethereum core clients to frontend libraries. This case study examines the model’s mechanics, its impact on the Ethereum ecosystem, and key lessons for future funding systems.

### The Problem
Open source maintainers face chronic underfunding. Donations are sporadic, grants are cumbersome, and corporate sponsorship is narrow. Gitcoin set out to prove that a decentralized matching pool could fairly amplify small contributions, aligning incentives across builders, users, and stakeholders.

### The Quadratic Funding Solution
Instead of a simple 1:1 match, Gitcoin Grants used the formula:

> **Matching for project i** = (∑√cᵢ)²

Where cᵢ are each individual’s contribution. This mathematically ensures:
- Broad support beats large whales
- The influence of a $100 contribution is equivalent to ten $10 contributions
- The matching pool is allocated efficiently (in theory, it maximizes plurality utility)

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #6

**Severity:** High  
**Component:** Core Base Layer  
**File:** `cashu/core/base.py` (line 324)  
**Impact:** Data manipulation, potential code execution  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925, #926 (same researcher)

---

## Summary

The core base layer uses `json.loads()` on database row data without proper validation, potentially allowing attackers to manipulate critical financial data.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/core/base.py`  
**Line:** 324

```python
outputs = None
if "outputs" in row.keys() and row["outputs"]:
    outputs = json.loads(row["outputs"])  # ← VULNERABLE

return cls(
    quote=row["quote"],
    method=row["method"],
    request=row["request"],
```

### The Bug

Database-stored JSON is deserialized without validation. If an attacker can manipulate database contents, they could:
1. Inject malicious serialized data
2. Manipulate transaction outputs
3. Potentially exploit deserialization vulnerabilities

---

## Impact

**Severity:** High

**Potential Impact:**
- Transaction output manipulation
- Financial data tampering
- Potential for code execution (depending on JSON content)

**Affected Users:**
- All wallet users
- Mint operators

---

## Recommended Fix

```python
outputs = None
if "outputs" in row.keys() and row["outputs"]:
    try:
        outputs_data = json.loads(row["outputs"])
        # Validate structure
        if not isinstance(outputs_data, list):
            raise ValueError("outputs must be a list")
        # Validate each output
        for output in outputs_data:
            if not isinstance(output, dict) or "amount" not in output:
                raise ValueError("Invalid output format")
        outputs = outputs_data
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        logger.error(f"Invalid JSON in outputs: {e}")
```

Hi **@faizzyhon**,

First off, thank you for developing **BUGSCANNER**. It’s a powerful tool for **PHP web security** and **bug bounty hunting**.

To make the tool even more accessible and versatile, I would like to suggest the implementation of a GUI mode. While the CLI is efficient for automation, a visual interface would provide several benefits:

- Ease of Use: Allows users who are less comfortable with the terminal to leverage the scanner’s power.

- Real-time Visualization: A dashboard to view scan progress, categorized vulnerabilities (Critical, High, Medium, Low), and live logs.

- Configuration Management: A visual way to toggle specific scan modules or input target lists without managing complex flags.

Implementation Ideas:

- A lightweight desktop framework like PyQt or Tkinter (if moving toward a Python-based wrapper).

- Alternatively, a Web-based UI (using a local Flask or FastAPI server) that displays the results in a browser.

I believe this addition would significantly increase the project's adoption within the security community. I’d love to hear your thoughts on this or if you'd be open to community contributions specifically for a UI layer.

Keep up the great work!

title: [Security #8] Early Return in Mint Tasks Skips Validation (Medium-High)
body:
---
title: "Security #8: Early Return in Mint Tasks Skips Validation (Medium-High)"
labels: ["security", "bug", "mint", "validation", "tasks"]
assignees: []
---

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #8

**Severity:** Medium-High  
**Component:** Mint Background Tasks  
**File:** `cashu/mint/tasks.py` (line 53)  
**Impact:** Payment processing bypass, state inconsistency  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925, #926, #927 (same researcher)

---

## Summary

The mint's background task for processing invoice callbacks returns early when a quote is not found, without proper validation or state management.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/mint/tasks.py`  
**Line:** 53

```python
async def _process_invoice_callback(
    checking_id: str,
    conn: Connection | None = None,
):
    # ...
    quote = await get_quote_byCheckId(
        checking_id=checking_id, db=self.db, conn=conn
    )
    if not quote:
        logger.error(f"Quote not found for {checking_id}")
        return  # ← VULNERABLE: Early return without proper handling
```

### The Bug

When a quote is not found:
1. Function logs an error
2. **Returns immediately**
3. **No state update**
4. **No cleanup**
5. **No notification**

This could lead to inconsistent mint state and potential race conditions.

---

## Impact

**Severity:** Medium-High

**Potential Impact:**
- Payment processing bypass
- Mint state inconsistency
- Race condition exploitation
- Potential double-spend

**Affected Users:**
- Mint operators
- Wallet users

---

## Recommended Fix

```python
if not quote:
    logger.error(f"Quote not found for {checking_id}")
    # Update state to reflect error
    await mark_invoice_as_failed(checking_id)
    # Notify administrators
    await notify_admin(f"Invalid checking_id: {checking_id}")
    return
```

---

**Requested Payout:** Cashu ecash or Lightning Bitcoin  
**Estimated Value:** $500-$2,000 (Medium-High severity)

title: Atropos integration ($2500 bounty)
body:
Hey! Nous Research here - we're the authors of an LLM RL environments repo called [atropos](https://github.com/NousResearch/atropos) which is designed to provide rollouts for multi-environment runs, and where each individual env can be single-turn, multi-turn, or multi-agent, R1-zero style, or have a custom chat template. Furthermore, environments can define token-level advantages and so are not necessarily tied to the same RL training algorithm.

Each environment is a self-contained microservice and we have an API that sequesters rollouts from all of the environments. A trainer can request a batch of data from the API. Here's our writeup of our [API spec](https://github.com/NousResearch/atropos/tree/main/atroposlib/api). 

Here's [an example integration into Axolotl](https://github.com/axolotl-ai-cloud/plugin-atropos) from the Axolotl authors, and our own [minimal example trainer](https://github.com/NousResearch/atropos/tree/main/example_trainer).

===========================
EDIT:

- The integration must use GRPO (but support the optional token-level advantage overrides when provided.). 
Please expose the usual verl configurables as in [this example](https://github.com/volcengine/verl/blob/main/examples%2Fgrpo_trainer%2Frun_qwen2_5-3b_gsm8k_grpo_lora.sh).
- Atropos doesn't spin up inference servers - we need verl to spin these servers up and then provide their endpoints to Atropos.
- verl should also manage policy weight updates to the inference server.
- Both the verl team and Nous team must approve your PR. 

===========================

We are offering a bounty of $2500 for an online RL integration, paid out at the time the PR is merged into verl main.

If there are multiple competing integrations we reserve the right to select the one we deem highest quality.

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**Enterprise-Grade Bug Report**

**Contract:** ERC6909
**Version:** 0.8.0
**Audited On:** 21 March 2026

**Introduction:**
This audit was conducted on the provided Solmate-based ERC6909 contract implementation. The audit focused on identifying potential critical and high-severity security vulnerabilities.

**Findings:**

### Critical Severity

1. **Reentrancy Vulnerability** in transferFrom function:
The `transferFrom` function uses a conditional statement to check if the sender has sufficient allowance to transfer the specified amount. However, this check is performed before updating the `balanceOf` and `allowance` mappings. This allows a malicious user to repeatedly call `transferFrom` in a loop, each time reducing the sender's allowance, ultimately causing a reentrancy attack.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, reentrancy-safe operations should be performed within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.

2. **Arithmetic Overflow Vulnerability** in balanceOf and allowance mappings:
The contract uses mappings to store balance and allowance data. However, the data type used for this mapping (uint256) may cause arithmetic overflows when the balances or allowance amounts approach the maximum limit of uint256.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using data types with a larger maximum value or using libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely, such as SafeMath or OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library.

### High Severity

1. **Missing Input Validation** in transfer and transferFrom functions:
The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions lack proper input validation. Specifically, they do not check if the `receiver` address is not the zero address (0x0).

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add input validation to check for zero addresses and other invalid conditions.

2. **Incorrect Gas Optimizations in setOperator function:**
The `setOperator` function uses a mapping to store operator status, which can lead to excessive storage growth and associated gas costs over time.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using a Merkle tree or other gas-efficient data structures to store operator status.

3. **Missing Authorization Checks** in certain functions:
Functions like `approve` and `transfer` lack proper authorization checks.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add authorization checks to ensure that only authorized parties can execute these functions.

**Recommendations:**

To address these vulnerabilities, consider the following recommendations:

1. Apply reentrancy-safe operations within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.
2. Use data types with a larger maximum value or libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely.
3. Implement input validation for zero addresses and other invalid conditions in all functions.

title: $0.4 Bounty : Login fails with "Invalid Email" after successful signup and OTP verification
body:
### Description
After completing the signup process successfully, login does not work with the same email and password.
The app shows an **"Invalid Email"** error during login.

### Signup Flow
1. User signs up using:
   - Name
   - Email
   - Phone number
   - Password
2. After signup, the app redirects to **phone number OTP verification**
3. OTP is entered correctly and verified
4. Account is created successfully

### Issue
After logging out, when trying to log in using the **same email and password** used during signup,
the login fails and shows **"Invalid Email"** error.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the app
2. Go to Signup screen
3. Enter name, email, phone number, and password
4. Verify phone number using OTP
5. Logout after successful signup
6. Go to Login screen
7. Enter the same email and password
8. Login fails with "Invalid Email" error

### Expected Behavior
User should be able to log in successfully using the same email and password after signup.

### Actual Behavior
Login fails with "Invalid Email" error even though the account was created successfully.

### Platform
- Java (Android)
- Email & Phone OTP Authentication

### Additional Information
- OTP verification works correctly
- Signup flow completes without any error
- Issue happens only during login after signup

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**BUG REPORT**

**Contract Name:** ERC6909
**Severity:** HIGH
**Report Date:** March 21, 2026

**Summary:**
The ERC6909 contract is vulnerable to several high-severity bugs, including unguarded transfers, approval bypassing, and operator mismanagement. These vulnerabilities can be exploited by malicious users to drain assets, steal approvals, and manipulate operator status.

**Vulnerabilities:**

1. **Unprotected Transfer**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transfer` function does not check if the sender has sufficient balance before performing the transfer. This allows an attacker to transfer more assets than they own, potentially draining the contract's assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `transfer` with a malicious `amount` parameter, exceeding their own balance.
	* **Recommendation:** Add a balance check before performing the transfer: `require(balanceOf[msg.sender][id] >= amount, "Insufficient balance");`
2. **Approval Bypass**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transferFrom` function does not properly check the allowance when the sender is not the same as the message sender. This allows an attacker to bypass the approval mechanism and steal assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `transferFrom` with a malicious `sender` parameter, bypassing the approval check.
	* **Recommendation:** Update the allowance check to account for the sender: `if (msg.sender != sender && !isOperator[sender][msg.sender]) { ... }` should be `if (msg.sender != sender && (allowance[sender][msg.sender][id] < amount || !isOperator[sender][msg.sender])) { ... }`
3. **Operator Mismanagement**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `setOperator` function allows an operator to be set without proper authorization. This can lead to unauthorized access to assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `setOperator` to grant themselves operator status without the owner's consent.
	* **Recommendation:** Add a check to ensure only the owner can set operators: `require(msg.sender == owner, "Only the owner can set operators");`
4. **Reentrancy Vulnerability**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions emit events, which can lead to reentrancy attacks. An attacker can exploit this to drain assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can create a contract that listens for the `Transfer` event and reenters the `transfer` or `transferFrom` function.
	* **Recommendation:** Use a reentrancy lock or a more secure pattern, such as the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern.

title: ## 🚀 Feature Request: Real-Time Bounty Activity Signals
body:
### Problem
Currently, it’s difficult for contributors to evaluate whether a bounty is active, trustworthy, or worth investing time in. Many bounties appear inactive or abandoned, which reduces engagement and confidence in the platform.

### Proposed Solution
Introduce real-time activity indicators for each bounty to improve transparency and decision-making.

### Key Features
- 👥 Number of Active Contributors  
  Show how many contributors are currently working on or have claimed the bounty.

- 🕒 Last Activity Timestamp  
  Display the most recent activity (e.g., issue comment, commit, PR update).

- 🔀 PRs Submitted  
  Show the number of pull requests linked to the bounty.

- 🔥 “Hot / Active Bounty” Badge  
  Automatically label bounties as “Hot” or “Active” based on recent activity signals (e.g., frequent updates, multiple contributors, recent PRs).

### Expected Impact
- Helps contributors quickly identify active and promising bounties  
- Increases trust and platform transparency  
- Improves bounty completion rate  
- Encourages more engagement on active issues  

### Optional Enhancements
- Activity trend graph (last 24h / 7 days)  
- Notifications for activity spikes  
- Sorting/filtering by activity level  

### Notes
This feature could leverage existing GitHub integration data (issues, PRs, commits) to calculate activity signals in near real-time.

title: Tags page: clicking sort headers adds an extra filter/search field
body:
### Mautic Series 7.0.x series ### Mautic installed version 7.0.1 ### Way of installing I installed with composer using https://github.com/mautic/recommended-project ### PHP version 8.2.29 ### What browsers are you seeing the problem on? _No response_ ### What happened? Summary On the Tags page, clicking a sortable column header such as "Name" or "ID" triggers sorting, but it also causes an additional filter/search field to appear at the top of the table. ### How can we reproduce this issue? Steps to reproduce 1. Go to Tags 2. Open the tags list 3. Click the "Name" column header or the "ID" column header Actual result The list is sorted, but an extra filter/search field appears above the table. Expected result The list should sort normally without adding another filter/search field. ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### Code of Conduct - [x] I confirm that I have read and agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

title: Issue 202: chore: Define Standardized GitHub PR and Issue Templates
body:
## Description
To manage external contributions effectively during the bounty program, we need strict templates for bug reports, feature requests, and pull requests to ensure maintainers get all necessary context upfront.

## Requirements
- [ ] Create `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` requiring Node version, SDK version, and a minimum reproducible example.
- [ ] Create `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature_request.md` focusing on the proposed API signature and use case.
- [ ] Create `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` with a mandatory checklist (tests passed, docs updated, linted).
